title: CI Account Deploying into Prod
description:
## Cross-Account Deployment Without Shared Keys

A CI/CD pipeline runs in a dedicated **tooling account**. It must deploy infrastructure and application updates into a separate **production account**. Security policy forbids storing **long-lived credentials** of the production account anywhere in the tooling account, and every deployment action must be **auditable** and use **short-lived** credentials.

What is the correct cross-account access pattern?

options.text: In the **production** account, create an IAM **role** with the needed deploy permissions and a **trust policy** allowing the tooling account's pipeline principal to **`sts:AssumeRole`**; the pipeline assumes it to get temporary credentials per deployment.
Create an IAM **user** in the production account, generate its access keys, and store them as secrets in the tooling account's pipeline.
Make the production account's resources **public** and restrict by source IP so the tooling account can deploy over the internet.
Set up **VPC peering** between the two accounts; network connectivity automatically grants the pipeline permission to deploy resources.

explanation: The standard, secure cross-account pattern is **IAM role assumption**. The **resource-owning account (production)** creates a role whose **trust policy** names the **tooling account** (or a specific role/principal in it) as allowed to call **`sts:AssumeRole`**, and attaches the deploy permissions to that role. The pipeline assumes the role and receives **temporary STS credentials** scoped to the role — no long-lived production keys ever leave production, and every assume-role and API call is recorded in **CloudTrail** for audit. Option B reintroduces exactly the prohibited **long-lived credentials**. Option C is a gross security violation — deployment is an IAM/control-plane concern, not something solved by making resources public. Option D confuses **network connectivity with authorization**: VPC peering only routes packets between VPCs and grants **no IAM permissions** to deploy anything.

hints.title: The owner defines the trust
hints.body: Cross-account access is set up from the resource side: prod creates the role and trusts the other account; the other account assumes it.

hints.title: Network != permission
hints.body: Connectivity (peering/TGW) never grants IAM permissions. Authorization is always identity/role-based.
